tooHigh: Diets high in saturated fat tend to raise LDL cholesterol and are linked with higher cardiovascular risk when they displace unsaturated fats.
Major health groups recommend keeping saturated fat moderate and swapping toward mono- and polyunsaturated sources.
enough: Some saturated fat is normal in whole foods (dairy, meat, coconut); balance matters more than zero intake.
Pairing saturated fat with fiber-rich plants and unsaturated oils supports a heart-healthier overall pattern.

tooLow: Very low monounsaturated intake often means relying on refined carbs or saturated fats instead of olive- or avocado-style fats.
Mediterranean-style eating patterns linked to heart health emphasize this fat class.
enough: Monounsaturated fats support HDL, help replace saturated and trans fats, and fit well in stable cooking oils.
They add satiety without the inflammatory profile of some refined seed-oil-heavy processed foods.

tooLow: Too little polyunsaturated fat can mean inadequate essential omega-3 and omega-6 from food.
Very low-fat diets sometimes reduce absorption of fat-soluble vitamins.
enough: Polyunsaturated fats include essential fatty acids the body cannot make in needed amounts.
Balance between omega-3 and omega-6 matters more than total PUFA alone.

tooHigh: Industrial trans fats raise LDL, lower HDL, and increase heart disease risk; there is no safe intake level recommended.
Partially hydrogenated oils in older packaged foods were the main source; still check labels for "partially hydrogenated."
enough: Near-zero industrial trans fat is the goal; tiny amounts can occur naturally in dairy and meat.
Avoiding deep-fried and highly processed baked goods keeps intake minimal.

tooHigh: High dietary cholesterol may raise LDL in some people, especially with low fiber and high saturated fat overall.
For many, saturated and trans fats affect blood lipids more than cholesterol from eggs and shellfish alone.
enough: Dietary cholesterol supports cell membranes and hormone synthesis; needs are partly met by the body.
Whole-food sources (eggs, shellfish) differ from cholesterol paired with processed meats.

tooLow: Low omega-3 intake is linked with higher inflammation markers and weaker cardiovascular support.
Plant ALA converts poorly to EPA/DHA; fish or algae sources matter for direct EPA/DHA.
enough: Omega-3 fats support heart rhythm, triglycerides, brain structure, and resolution of inflammation.
Combine fatty fish, walnuts, flax, or algae oil based on diet preference.

tooHigh: Modern diets often oversupply omega-6 from refined seed oils, skewing the omega-6 : omega-3 ratio.
High omega-6 relative to omega-3 may favor pro-inflammatory eicosanoids when not balanced.
enough: Linoleic acid is essential; the issue is usually excess processed oils, not whole nuts and seeds.
Lowering omega-6-heavy oils while raising omega-3 improves the ratio without eliminating omega-6.

tooLow: Low omega-9 is uncommon; the body can make oleic acid, but food sources add easy unsaturated fat.
Diets very low in all fats may lack oleic-rich staples like olive oil.
enough: Omega-9 (oleic acid) is non-essential but supports replacing saturated fat in cooking.
Olive-oil-forward patterns align with Mediterranean cardiometabolic benefits.

tooLow: Low ALA means fewer plant-based omega-3 building blocks; conversion to EPA/DHA is limited.
Vegans and low-nut/seed diets should plan ALA or direct EPA/DHA from algae.
enough: ALA from flax, chia, and walnuts contributes to omega-3 status alongside or instead of fish.
A few grams daily of ALA-rich foods supports essential fatty acid intake.

tooLow: Low EPA limits anti-inflammatory marine omega-3 effects and may leave triglycerides and mood support under-addressed.
Fish-oil and fatty-fish intake are the main food sources.
enough: EPA supports cardiovascular health, inflammation balance, and is often paired with DHA in fish.
Regular fatty-fish meals or targeted fish oil supply meaningful EPA.

tooLow: Low DHA may affect brain and retina structure over time; important in pregnancy and aging.
Plant ALA alone often does not restore DHA without marine or algae sources.
enough: DHA is a major structural fat in the brain and eyes and supports cognitive and visual health.
Fatty fish and algae-based supplements provide direct DHA.

tooHigh: Excess linoleic acid from refined oils can dominate omega-6 intake and widen the omega-6 : omega-3 gap.
Ultra-processed foods fried in soybean or corn oil are common drivers.
enough: Linoleic acid is an essential omega-6; whole-food sources (nuts, seeds) differ from bottled oils used everywhere.
Aim for adequacy without high-omega-6 oils crowding out omega-3.

tooHigh: Arachidonic acid can feed pro-inflammatory pathways when omega-3 is low and overall diet is heavy in certain animal fats.
It is less commonly tracked than total omega-6 but appears in meat and eggs.
enough: Some arachidonic acid is normal in omnivorous diets; balance with omega-3 and whole plants matters.
Not essential to maximize; focus on ratio and food quality.

tooLow: Low oleic acid often reflects little olive or avocado oil use—not usually a clinical deficiency.
Replacing butter or palm oil with oleic-rich oils improves fat quality.
enough: Oleic acid is the main omega-9 in olive oil and supports heart-healthy fat substitution.
Stable for cooking compared with high-PUFA oils.

tooHigh: Palmitoleic acid (omega-7) in very high amounts from processed or overfed animal products is an emerging research area.
Less commonly tracked; whole-diet pattern matters more than single fatty acids.
enough: Omega-7 occurs in some fish, macadamia oil, and sea buckthorn; not an essential fat to target high.
Optional tracking for completeness in longevity-focused diets.

tooLow: Low vitamin E reduces antioxidant protection for cell membranes and LDL particles.
Very low-fat diets or fat malabsorption increase risk.
enough: Vitamin E protects fats from oxidation and supports immune and skin health.
Nuts, seeds, and oils are the best food sources; needs rise with high PUFA intake.

tooLow: Low vitamin K can impair clotting factors and osteocalcin activity, affecting bone and vascular calcium handling.
Antibiotics, warfarin, or very low green vegetable intake increase risk.
enough: Vitamin K supports blood clotting and helps direct calcium toward bone rather than soft tissues.
K1 from greens and K2 from fermented foods and some animal products play overlapping roles.

tooLow: Low K1 (phylloquinone) mainly affects clotting and bone proteins when green vegetable intake is very low.
Consistent leafy greens usually cover K1.
enough: K1 from plants supports clotting and works with vitamin D and calcium for bone metabolism.
A serving of leafy greens most days is a simple baseline.

tooLow: Low K2 (menaquinone) is debated for bone and arterial health, especially with high calcium and low K overall.
Fermented foods and animal products supply most dietary K2.
enough: K2 may help activate proteins that regulate where calcium deposits; research is strongest in deficiency and CKD contexts.
Natto, cheese, and egg yolks are notable sources.

tooLow: Low selenium weakens antioxidant enzymes (glutathione peroxidase), thyroid conversion, and immunity.
Soil-poor regions and very restrictive diets increase risk.
enough: Selenium supports thyroid hormone activation, DNA repair, and oxidative stress defense.
A few Brazil nuts weekly or seafood and eggs often cover needs—avoid megadoses.

tooLow: Low polyphenol intake means fewer plant antioxidants that support gut microbes, blood vessels, and inflammation balance.
Diets low in berries, tea, cocoa, and herbs miss easy sources.
enough: Polyphenols from diverse plants add color, flavor, and antioxidant/anti-inflammatory potential beyond vitamins.
No single DV exists—variety and whole foods are the practical goal.

tooLow: Low flavonoid intake parallels low fruit, tea, and vegetable diversity—linked with weaker vascular support in population studies.
Highly processed diets often lack these compounds entirely.
enough: Flavonoids (a polyphenol subclass) support endothelial function and may modestly lower blood pressure in higher intakes.
Citrus, berries, tea, and onions are reliable contributors.

tooLow: Low carotenoids reduce beta-carotene, lutein, and lycopene intake—important for eyes, skin, and antioxidant networks.
Very low produce intake is the usual cause.
enough: Carotenoids from orange, red, and green plants support vision (lutein/zeaxanthin) and general antioxidant status.
Fat with meals improves absorption.

tooLow: No essential deficiency—low curcumin simply means less turmeric in cooking and fewer curry-style anti-inflammatory phytochemicals.
Whole turmeric with black pepper and fat improves absorption vs. spice dust alone.
enough: Curcumin from turmeric is studied for joint comfort and inflammation markers; food amounts are smaller than trial doses.
Regular culinary turmeric plus pepper is a low-risk way to include it.

tooLow: Not an essential nutrient—low resveratrol reflects little red grapes, berries, or peanuts in the diet.
Wine is not required; grapes and berries supply it without alcohol.
enough: Resveratrol is a polyphenol studied for aging pathways; food doses are far below many supplement trials.
Whole grapes, blueberries, and peanuts contribute modest amounts.

tooLow: CoQ10 is made in the body; low food intake rarely causes deficiency except with statins or certain diseases.
Older age and some medications lower tissue CoQ10.
enough: CoQ10 supports mitochondrial energy and may help when depleted (e.g., on statins).
Organ meats and fatty fish provide small dietary amounts.

tooLow: No classical deficiency—low sulforaphane means infrequent cruciferous vegetables, missing easy detox-support phytochemicals.
Chopping broccoli and letting it sit before cooking boosts sulforaphane formation.
enough: Sulforaphane from broccoli sprouts and crucifers activates cellular antioxidant defenses (Nrf2).
Several weekly servings of broccoli, kale, or cabbage are practical targets.

tooHigh: Excess phosphorus—especially phosphate additives in processed foods—may harm bone–mineral balance and vascular health in susceptible people.
CKD patients must limit phosphorus carefully; most people should not chase higher intake.
Food labels listing "phosphate" or "phosphoric acid" are red flags.
enough: Phosphorus is abundant in whole foods; deficiency is rare without medical issues.
Adequate protein, dairy, and legumes usually cover needs without additives.

tooHigh: Frequent high-GI choices spike blood glucose quickly and can increase glycemic load when portions are large.
White bread, sugary drinks, and refined cereals are common high-GI staples.
enough: Lower-GI carbs digest slower, smoothing glucose and insulin; pairing carbs with fat, protein, or acid lowers effective GI.
GI is per food—portion size and GL matter as much as the number.
foodSources: Low: beans, pasta al dente, apples
Medium: oats, sweet potato, brown rice
High: white bread, rice cakes, candy

tooHigh: High added sugar drives glycemic load, liver fat, triglycerides, and dental issues; linked with weight gain when calories exceed needs.
Soda, sweets, and sweetened yogurt are concentrated sources beyond whole fruit.
enough: Keeping added sugar modest leaves room for whole fruit and stable energy.
WHO suggests limiting free sugars for cardiometabolic health; labels list "added sugars" separately.

tooHigh: Heavy refined carb intake (white flour, stripped grains) raises glycemic load and often displaces fiber and micronutrients.
Frequent spikes can worsen insulin resistance over time in susceptible people.
enough: Whole grains and intact grains digest slower and carry more fiber and minerals.
Mixing refined carbs with protein, fat, and vegetables blunts glucose response.

tooHigh: High net carbs (total carbs minus fiber) mean more absorbable carbohydrate per meal—relevant for keto tracking and glycemic control.
Large net-carb loads behave like high GL even if fiber is present elsewhere in the day.
enough: Net carbs help compare portions when fiber is high; whole foods with fiber lower net carbs per serving.
Use alongside GL/GI for a fuller carb-quality picture.
foodSources: Fiber lowers net in whole grains

tooHigh: Ratios well above 4:1 (often 10:1+ in Western diets) suggest omega-6-heavy oils dominating omega-3 intake.
Shifting oils toward olive oil and adding fish or flax improves the ratio without eliminating omega-6.
enough: A lower omega-6 : omega-3 ratio is associated with less inflammatory signaling in research models.
Track EPA/DHA and linoleic acid sources, not just totals.

tooHigh: A high saturated : unsaturated ratio mirrors butter, palm, and fatty meat displacing olive oil, nuts, and fish.
Heart-health guidelines emphasize replacing saturated with unsaturated fats.
enough: More unsaturated fat relative to saturated supports healthier lipid profiles in most people.
Cook with olive or avocado oil; keep saturated fat from whole foods in moderation.
foodSources: Lower ratio: olive oil, fish, nuts
Higher ratio: butter, cheese, palm oil

tooLow: Low combined EPA + DHA limits direct marine omega-3 benefits for heart and brain.
Flax alone may not raise EPA/DHA blood levels enough for many people.
enough: EPA and DHA together are the active omega-3s from fish and algae; sum them for weekly seafood goals.
Rough targets often cite ~250–500 mg/day combined from food or supplements for general adults.

tooHigh: High daily glycemic load means frequent large portions of high-GI carbs—white rice, bread, sweets, and drinks.
GL = GI × carbs per serving ÷ 100; watermelon can be high GI but lower GL in small portions.
enough: Moderate GL smooths glucose curves and supports steady energy.
Prioritize GL over GI alone because portion size determines real-world impact.
